author: @TimeSurfer206
text:
I have been interested in steam for some time, as it is easy to make steam. I did cut firewood for many years...
But, the "Boom" factor is not so intriguing. 
Thus, I am actually looking at the Destructive Distillation of wood, and gasification, to feed a standard Infernal Combustion Generator, with all of Wood Gas, Methanol, and Charcoal. And use its hot exhaust to heat a plate with a few NASA Stirling Engines on it, with linear generators on them, OR as the heat source for a retrofitted Gas Water Heater (or both), which said water heater shall be both domestic hot water and for the radiators to be installed in my Caravan.
Methane Production is also intriguing, but, storage does present that BOOM risk, as well.
Taking out the neighbour's windows when I take off my roof might make the neighbours a bit miffed.

replies.author: @hootsmin
replies.text: Tinfoil is probably about the cheapest option I would think, you will want to ground it, use a few layers.  You should save for a shielded sleeping bag, some 40% silver clothes and tins of y-shield paint, in my opinion and I have the EMF sickness so I know what I am talking about, also I have a faraday room and a shielded bag for sleeping in and I am now working on clothing (wears hoodie all day long, climbs in bag wearing hoodie, sleeps in faraday cage painted room).  First things first though, you need a good EMF meter and a power field/magnetic field meter or a tri-field meter that reads the lot so you can see where the problem areas are, magnetic fields and power fields are very dangerous, don't cheap out on the meters.  Don't consume too much calcium, supplement magnesium in high dose, swell arteries with ginkgo biloba and work on your digestion.  Look up Rouleaux, that's what you are basically trying to treat, that's what your blood cells do when the EMR hits them.

replies.author: @hootsmin
replies.text: Watch the ginkgo if your on blood medication or anything, seek medical advice if uncertain, I'm not a medical doctor.  I am however, my own doctor, so I take it everyday, the Chinese monks used to use it to enhance their mental powers.

Good luck with the project but please stress safety as a primary requirement, steam boiler explosions

author: @rogerbraintree9552
text:
Well here's another idea to throw in.  A plastic steam engine.  Acrylic for the cylinder and resin for the piston.  Use the acrylic cylinder bore to mould the resin piston.  Because the operating temperature is much lower than for an internal combustion engine, you're only dealing with temperatures up to around 140 C for a low pressure engine, the whole thing can be made from plastic and wood.  The valves can also be plastic.  One advantage of plastic is it's a good insulator which means the steam won't cool down as it transfers from boiler to cylinder.  It also means the whole thing could arguably be made without a lathe.

It also brings in the prospect of 3d printing parts from ABS.  Certainly mouldings can be 3d printed.  A plastic steam engine would certainly be light weight.

He is on a hiatus now through, building a workshop to continue working in.

author: @OffGridUrbanist
text: Be careful. I live off grid with a 5hp boiler and a 4hp steam engine. Both were commissioned by an experienced Boiler Maker. Steam is dangerous. It combines the danger of compressed air and hydraulic systems. Spring loaded heat. To be borderline economical for power generation you are looking at a boiler pressure of 80-150 psi for a small system. Fabricating safe steam pressure vessels is an intense undertaking.

replies.author: @twestgard2
replies.text:
@realfamilyman I’ve dealt personally with overly intrusive government idiots, and it is a real problem. That said, when you know the amount of damage that even a small steam boiler can cause if it’s not built correctly and maintained, it’s probably a good thing that there’s some effort to regulate them.

Where I am, in rural Indiana, I can build and install a steam boiler up to ten gallons without any approvals or inspections whatsoever, but that’s only true for individuals at their own homes. I might do it for myself, but I’m kinda glad that it’s not legal for anybody to go around installing steam boilers all over.

replies.author: @darrylpang3903
replies.text: An improperly built steam boiler is a bomb. There are a lot of regulations around restricting steam boilers to avoid people building bombs into their homes.

replies.author: @twestgard2
replies.text: @darrylpang3903 exactly, and even a little one can do a lot of damage.

replies.author: @hawnsl100
replies.text: I would buy a properly engineered and built heater, boiler and generator as a single unit.  I already have each as seperate units requiring separate input energy.

author: @brianmurphy8790
text:
I'd be using an old 4-stoke motorbike engine. I'd cut the camshaft in half and weld it back together, with different timing. Then I'd put 1-1 gearing from crank to cam.
That's it, then I'd feed high pressure steam into the inlet port of the engine. 
There needs to be some method to keep the cylinders well oiled/greased. If not, the rings/valves/bore will begin to rust right after first use. I'd probably just use soluble oil - the stuff that turns white when mixed with water. The oil doesn't separate from the steam, it's carried with it. Some will condense in the engine, keep it lubed and stop rust. 
With a good steam recovery system the same water/oil could be used repeatedly.

author: @Nuts-Bolts
text:
The reference to the WW2 engine reminds of: "Ricardo's Steam Engine, 1951. (This engine was a twin)
It was built in conjunction with the National Research and Development Corporation (the purpose of which is to finance likely inventions) with a view, to selling it on the Indian market. The idea was that the Indian village community should have some other source of power than bullocks, and this engine had a boiler which could burn any fuel including brushwood and camel dung." -copied & pasted from the Sir Harry Ricardo F.R.S. website.

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: That's one thing I never understood about boilers and steam turbines - how do they supply fresh water to it under pressure? How do they keep the steam from backflowing into the tank or the supply line? And how much energy does that use compared to the output? I guess that's not a problem if you just have a sealed tank that gets consumed, and then manually open and refill it, but for things like power plants I think it's a closed loop.

text: For fuel I'd like you to try in the rocket stove fallen autumn leaves (crushed into briquettes), acorns, honeycombs and conkers. Also sawdust. Which is the best free waste unwanted fuel that is plentyful?

replies.author: @twestgard2
replies.text: Plastic, but you need a pyrolysis chamber. Not all plastics work well and especially no PVC because of the dioxin. Use a steam jet to reduce volume of things like cling wrap and bottles before they go in the chamber.

replies.author: @debbiehenri345
replies.text:
Pine cones and Eucalyptus bark/twigs/dried leaves would be very good here. Both burn hot, but Eucalyptus ferociously so. 

I grow several Eucalyptus trees in my garden and some don't mind coppicing, regrowing to supply fuel for another year (unless there's a really severe winter like the -20C winters we had some years ago. That tends to kill them off and I then have to start again).
I grow the Eucalyptus mainly because they drive away some of the flies from the neighbouring farm (the nickname for one species being Shoo-Fly), but they have been an asset when it comes to firewood production because most hardy species grow fast and it burns for longer than any other native wood.

author: @stevenfaber3896
text: Just thought of something, to aid your fuel entry setup on the stove; can one shove the activated carbon felt down the length, prior to coiling the bends, and then put it together with the drip fashion into the rocket stove? I think that may act as a nice filtration system for use of used motor oil, and something where you can split it into 2, a length that's removable at the top end, and one that's permanently housed within the coil? then one simply needs a ramrod and a bit of patience every 3 months or so, and you can begin to assist your buddies over at the auto shop in one of their least favorite things to pay for I'm sure, used oil removal.

author: @richardlingham9400
text: Hi rob, have seen a similar idea before but not on a rocket stove. One improvement I can think of is running a pipe in the chimney that the steam from the boiler then runs through to super heat the steam. Would greatly increase efficiency as you would be using the heat scourge twice in effect. Also lagging the boiler with a sand jacket increasing the thermal mass might be a good idea

replies.author: @richardlingham9400
replies.text: @manoo422 I like your idea and it would remove any soot remaining in the air stream. After that stage could add an inter cooler from a turbo car or van as a heat exchanger as a air heater. Biggest problem is removing all available heat energy adds complexity, maintenance and cost. Would be fantastic to heat your home, hot water and generate electricity from twigs

author: @shiftingsign
text: I'm excited to see what you come up with, It would be a great resource for me to learn what you would have to do to convert a compressor to a steam engine, I have a few fridge compressor just lying around and I've always thought of trying to make them into steam engine. The first problem I have is I cant find a good tutorial online on how to do that specifically with fridge compressors, and second I only have access to machining, what I got is a grinder, drill and a welding machine. I find your builds and tutorials really easy to follow and you always find ways to make it as simple as possible.

replies.author: @rogerbraintree9552
replies.text:
What about a plastic steam engine?  Acrylic for the cylinder and resin for the piston.  Use the acrylic cylinder bore to mould the resin piston.  Because the operating temperature is much lower than for an internal combustion engine, you're only dealing with temperatures up to around 140 C for a low pressure engine, the whole thing can be made from plastic and wood.  The valves can also be plastic.  One advantage of plastic is it's a good insulator which means the steam won't cool down as it transfers from boiler to cylinder.  It also means the whole thing could arguably be made without a lathe.

It also brings in the prospect of 3d printing parts from ABS.  Certainly mouldings can be 3d printed.  A plastic steam engine would certainly be light weight.
